sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after UP

for n in seg['nodes']:
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    print(f"Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}], children={n['children']}")

# Compare before/after for the transition
print("\nBefore UP:")
for n in last_transition.before_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see! After UP:
- The eyes moved UP: from rows 49-53 to rows 44-48 (moved up by 5)
- The black shape (node 4) now has node 5 as a child again (containment restored)
- The timer borders also changed
So the pattern is:
- LEFT/RIGHT: moves eyes horizontally
- UP/DOWN: moves eyes vertically
- The eyes move 5 pixels in the direction pressed
Let me now understand what the goal is. The character has two eyes that can be moved around. Maybe the goal is to position them at specific locations, or to make them meet, or to move them to specific colored regions.
Let me try DOWN to see if the eyes move down, and also try SPACE to see what it does.

<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try DOWN to move eyes back down, then check SPACE
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
